sentences.text: I was working like for the Polish.
They call this [POLISH].
The [POLISH], the Secretary of Forest.
They send out the-- the-- the-- the trees or they chop everything.
They send out, and I rented land by the railroad from the Polish government.
And I used to-- they used to send out from the-- from the forest, and I used to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and I used to put in the people where they brought those trees.
They used to-- I used to keep them over there.

And later on, I used to send them wherever they sold them, to Germany, to England, to north-- and I had-- and I had people, Ukrainian, probably I had about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 24 or about 30 people, and the same three girls, they were over there.
And they made those for the barrels for-- for the whiskey, for-- for England.
So they make smaller little-- little [NON-ENGLISH], where they called this for beer bar, for beer in Poland.
So I had girls, Ukrainian girls, when the people came, the people who bought it, the companies sent people to-- to-- to test them, because they used to check them if they are the proper and right.
So I had girls working for me.
And I used to get from them-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I used to get-- I get from them, not monthly, according to what I did, I used to get paid from that.
This was my job.
And next to the same, I had the one what-- what it was the big tank, what it was kerosene.
And my-- and my brother-in-law, he took care-- he took care of this.
When the war broke out, we were in Mizocz.
It so happened I wasn't in the Polish army, so the Soviet Union took me.
They mobilized me to the Soviet Union army, and they sent me to the front-- they sent me.
And then-- and the next day, I was a prisoner of war.
About 30 kilometers from our town.
It's about 20 miles from our town.
They took us all to this prison.
And it so happened, it's like I said before, it's destiny.
And so one-- one guy from the-- from the-- from the Jewish men, he says, let's run away.
And we weren't so watched.
So, five of us Jewish soldiers from this-- from the prisoners of war, we run away, and we went to the town.
The town had a lot of Jewish people, so they gave me civilian clothing and I--
Dubno.
Dubno, the town is Dubno.
It's-- it's even a famous town because there is over there, there was famous Dubno Market.
It's famous.
So later on I let know my father, they tie him here, so he sent the Ukrainian with a wagon and horses and brought me home.
And when the beginning, we lived in our house.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes, in the beginning, we lived in our house.
I was forced-- I don't know-- I was forced to go to Zdolbunow-- another town about-- about 10 miles from our town, and I had to work over there.
By the Germans.
I don't know exactly how-- how long I worked over there.
I got beaten up a few times because I was a heavy smoker.
We didn't have a smoke.
If I want time out, I picked up something, I beat.
He throw it away, and I want to pick up, so I got beat up by the Germans.
But I got beat up.
So I worked over there because they forced the town to send so many young people to work.
And I did probably my-- my share where how-- how long I had to be over there, so I came back to town.
Later they-- they took us out for a mile out.
They made a ghetto.
They made a ghetto and we had to leave our house and came in to the-- to the ghetto.
The ghetto was part of the town.
Part of the town they made a ghetto, part of the town.
And there came a order-- the Jewish people had to put on-- not-- not the-- they had to put on a yellow patch here, here, and in-- and in the back, a yellow patch.
But now I had to put on this yellow patch.
Believe me or not-- I got-- [CRYING] the whole truth open and swallowed me.
Because the most Ukrainian they weren't educated.
And they used to come to our house, they took off their hat, and it was a special way that-- we invite them.
We did it.
Yes, and the Jewish people didn't have the right to walk the sidewalks-- only where the horses and the cattles in the road, they-- they were walking.
And the Ukrainian girls, they were all laughing and everything.
Nobody can know or feel what I felt.
Yeah, this was the ghetto already.
You see, human-- a human being, when he commits a crime, when he get punished-- is against-- still he feels he did something wrong.
I didn't do nothing-- nothing wrong.
So that's why the pain was-- was hard, and they make me-- and they make me lower than a dirt.
I was lower than a dirt.
I kept asking why.
I heard the people working from me.
If they would be today alive they would say, you see, but I was 12 years old, or 13 years old-- my father never hit a child.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He told me, my son, if you want to be-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] if you are-- if-- if you want to be respected, respect all human beings, [SOBBING] regardless who he is.
And when we built the house, we built a big house, and we ran out of funds.
So my father told me, you want to get rich, you eat bread and water, and the people who work for you, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] paid in time.
And you see, in Europe, was-- you could buy-- the merchants could buy a lot of things, could buy on note-- they gave notes on certain-- they called this a [NON-ENGLISH].
They gave note.
See, we used to pour kerosene, you had to pay cash.
For oil, and benzene, like-- because we had to-- we had to order a barrel in our town.
Oh, yeah, we had in our town a big sugar factory, we had in our town.
So we had oil and everything to sell to them and gasoline.
And gasoline-- That's to the Ukrainians?
What?
No, no, no.
You see, the [POLISH]-- in Polish, it wasn't a baron.
It's a-- they called this a [POLISH].
It was here, he was near it.
He was a wealthy man, and he had a car-- he had cars.
Yeah.
And then there was in our town a big sugar factory.
They used to make sugar from beets.
So the factory did belong to a famous Jewish-- Brotzky they called him.
He had-- all over Russia, he had sugar factories.
And there was-- and he built our whole town especially for the sugar factory.
He built-- he-- he built a whole town.
So, and it so happened-- and it so happened, what I want to just show you what kind of a-- of a human being my father was.
So I-- so I told you for the oil-- for the-- for the ordered products, we-- my-- we gave notes.
And the notes came in, and you had to pay them.
So if you couldn't pay them, so we bankrupt-- we went bankrupt.
And in Poland, it was customary, they settle with you, 30%, 40%, they settled.
When we-- when we-- when we didn't pay the note, so they came from-- from those oil refineries, where they sell-- they came, and they want to settle with my father for 40%, 50%.
So my father told them, I never in my life took [CRYING] from nobody.
[CRYING] Do me only one favor-- give me two years to pay up, and I'll pay you with interest.
And he paid up the whole 100%.
[CRYING] He didn't want to take money for nothing.
He-- he wants store, and I'm going to get mixed up the same thing.
When I came to the-- to the States, do you know there is a joint.
They gave-- they put up the-- the-- all DPs-- they-- all the refugees.
I didn't want to take from them $0.05.
I told them with my-- [CRYING] with my 10 fingers, I'm going to earn a living for my family.
[SOBBING]
And I went-- yes.
No.
You see, now in our part of Poland, they didn't send-- not to the concentration camps.
They liquidated town-- town after town.
There was town after town on this part-- where the war natural ravines, they let it.
When they want, they forced the Jewish people to dig ravines.
And they forced them-- but I was told by the-- by the Ukrainians, I didn't see it.
They forced them to take off their clothing and they had to-- and they had to walk on the edge of these, and they were standing with machine guns and shooting.
Who was killed, was killed, who wasn't fell in, and on the top of them fell in other ones.
Our town-- you see, they say a man who is drowning, he will catch a straw to save-- to save-- to save himself.
I do not know why.
They told us they were left-- from all this part, this part was called the eastern part of Poland, [NON-ENGLISH], the eastern part of Poland.
We were told they were left three times.
And we were told Hitler decided the ones that are left, the Jews, they are left to show the world there are still Jews.
We were naive people.
So we-- so we were told he will save us.
Hitler will not kill this part.
He wants to have Jews.
So this was after Yom Kippur.
It was in September.
They came in one night, and they circled.
They get the SS, with the help of the Ukrainian-- the Ukrainian-- they were in the police with the help of the Ukrainians.
They circled the whole town, and they decided-- and they-- to kill everybody.
And it so happened in the night time.
My father came to me, and he told me to cut the Russian gold coins.
And he told me he had, you go on, if you can save yourself.
I don't want to save myself, probably I had sinned.
[CRYING] I'll take the punishment.
So in the night time, when it was dark, I ran away.
They were shooting at me, and it so happened I was lucky they didn't hit me.
So I ran away, and I was running and running and running.
And I didn't know where I came in at the woods.
I came in over there, and over there was a natural hole over there.
I don't know what you call this in English.
No, not a ditch.
They call it different in English.
I forgot the term.
I know.
Cave like.
So I went in over there and I was laying over there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What I was-- oh, yeah, I have to tell you.
You see, I forgot.
And the-- and this-- when we had the kerosene, I had to manage Ukrainian men who worked for me 20-- 15 or 20 years.
He was over there-- he was over there-- he used to pump out the system.
He used to fill up the barrels.
You see, we used to sell the kerosene by steel barrels.
Each barrel had 200 liters.
We used to sell to the-- to the stores.
And they sold the-- by the liter.
He was working for me, for us.
His name was oh, god, my god.
OK, I forgot.
I cannot recall.
A Ukraine, yeah.
And I gave him away before that they made the ghetto, I gave him away a lot of things of ours-- I gave them away.
And I also hid in his place-- I don't remember, 400, two-- two barrels or steel barrels, or-- or three.
I hid 400 or 600 liter of kerosene in his place.
He had a little [? farm ?] in his place.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was-- I was in the cave about a week.
I used to go out in the nighttime.
They were still the sugar beets, and some of them I used to eat.
Later I decided, I will-- I cannot be here.
So I walked to this-- to this guy, who worked for us 20 years.
And he took me in.
After I ran away, after a week in-- in hiding in this cave and eating only the-- the-- the beet.
So-- so I decided in the night time, I'll walk.
So I walked and it so happened, I came to him, and he saw me, he took me in.
The German, there was an order-- any Ukrainian or Polack who will keep a Jew-- who will save a Jew, they will kill the whole-- the whole family.
Just he took me in.
And not to be-- he shouldn't be irresponsible.
All the bottles, when they kept their straw, and everything, they were open.
So he put me in the bottle, I made a hole in this-- in this straw, and I was over there laying and he just, he gave me to eat.
So in case if they will catch me over there, he's not responsible.
He didn't know I'm over there.
He didn't know over there.
So in the beginning, I was in his place.
How did-- how did I go away from him?
Oh, I forgot to tell you.
There is a-- there is a Jewish-- yeah, I had to-- I-- I was forced to work in the sugar factory.
I was working outside when they brought in the cars, the railroad cars with the beets.
I mean, the factory did belong to the Soviet Union.
Now, it did belong to the Germans.
They took it over and the-- and-- and the beets, because the factory used to work about three months a year, and they didn't have no beets from-- from around the farms.
So they used to bring them by railroad cars from-- from other farms.
My job, I used to load them up in little wagons, and I used to bring them in over there into the factory.
And they used to wash them and chop them and everything.
And this was what I want to tell you, there is a-- there is a saying in Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH]-- God sends the healing before he sends the plague.
And this did happen to me.
I used to-- It was in December, so it was cold.
I used to work the shift in the night time, from 12 till 8 o'clock in the morning, I used to work the shift.
I didn't get paid for it.
I had to work for nothing.
I didn't get paid.
So I used to go in where they made the steam to drive the factory.
And over there was the guy who took-- who took care.
He used to throw the coal and make the steam.
He was a Ukrainian.
A Ukrainian, and--
It was-- so we were known there in the neighborhood.
The Gentiles know us, and everything.
We were-- we were known, because I-- because I-- I-- they brought all the-- all the-- all the Ukrainian know us.
So he had a feeling, I probably hid somewhere kerosene, and everybody was sitting in the dark.
There were no kerosene.
So when I went in over there to warm up-- so he told me, [NON-ENGLISH], he told me, do me a favor.
It's Christmas is coming.
We are sitting in the dark.
I would like to have the Christmas-- bring me a liter of kerosene.
I'll give you anything you want, anything you want.
So I decided he was a nice guy.
Is everything-- I went and I took from them-- from the-- from the guy who worked, and he had the barrels of kerosene, 2 liters, and I gave him-- oh, his name was Karpo.
I went in, I gave him, and I told them, have a-- have a happy and a nice Christmas, I don't want nothing.
For those 2 liters [SOBBING] of kerosene, he kept me.
He kept you in the barn?
After I don't know what had happened-- I had to leave the barn from the guy who worked for me.
I don't know what was that reason, so I had to go-- I had to go away.
So I went to the Karpo, and he kept me.
I went-- I went into the barn.
And I was--
Yes, and I was laying in the-- in the straw.
I don't know.
Everybody was killed.
My brother with the-- with his three kids, my mother and father, and my sisters, all of them, they were-- they were killed, all of them.
The next day-- The next day, they killed them all.
They killed them all-- the whole town.
They forced them-- I was told later by the Ukrainian-- they forced them to take off their-- their-- their clothing, and they had to walk on the-- on the edge of the ravine.
And they kept with machine guns, shooting and killing them.
Who was killed was killed, who wasn't, the other fell on the top of him, and he-- and he suffocated.
I don't know what it was.
I was only-- I was only told one thing.
The Ukrainian told me that they-- a few days [INAUDIBLE], the [INAUDIBLE] was moving, because probably they were still over there, people not killed-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] not killed.
I went through.
You see, I'm sitting here before you.
I went through, and I still cannot believe the human being can be so barbaric.
So, too, [SOBBING] a wild animal will not eat their own kind.
And here, human beings-- it's worse than eating-- are killing their own kind.
Human beings are worse than animals.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Yeah, and I don't know what it was.
I had to leave him.
I had to leave and I went to Karpo.
Oh, the first-- the man who worked for me was Homa-- his name was Homa.
He worked for us for 20 years, he worked for us.
I wouldn't-- I wouldn't praise myself.
The people who worked for me, if I could bring them here, they would tell you [CRYING] how I treat them.
And I want to tell you, and I want to tell you one, just give you an example.
There were girls working for us.
When I was in-- when I was in hiding, by this guy-- so they used to make their own, they called it [NON-ENGLISH], their own vodka.
So he got drunk.
He got-- he got drunk, and he-- and he told-- and when he told them, do you know who-- who is in my barn?
No, I was-- with this guy, I wasn't in the barn already.
I was in the beginning, you see, he had-- what?
We have to change the tape.
